subject: ap-world-history
theme: Between 1200 and 1450, three major trade networks — the Silk Roads, Indian Ocean trade, and trans-Saharan routes — connected Afro-Eurasia in an increasingly integrated system that transmitted goods, technologies, religions, and diseases at unprecedented scale, with the Mongol Empire serving as both the network's greatest enabler and most devastating threat.

core_concepts.title: Silk Roads & Caravanserai Infrastructure
core_concepts.detail: The Silk Roads were overland trade networks linking China to the Mediterranean through Central Asian oasis cities (Kashgar, Samarkand); caravanserais provided essential infrastructure, and camel caravans carried silk, porcelain, spices, and technologies across the routes.

core_concepts.title: Indian Ocean Monsoon Trade
core_concepts.detail: The Indian Ocean trade network was driven by predictable monsoon winds — ships sailed northeast from October to April, southwest from April to October — creating seasonal commercial rhythms that concentrated diverse merchant diaspora communities (Arab, Indian, Chinese, Swahili) in key port cities like Malacca and Kilwa.

core_concepts.title: Pax Mongolica & Eurasian Commerce
core_concepts.detail: The Pax Mongolica (c. 1270–1350) produced unprecedented Eurasian trade by unifying Central Asian territories under one political authority, standardizing commercial infrastructure, and providing safe-conduct guarantees for merchants — but at the cost of catastrophic initial conquests that killed tens of millions.

core_concepts.title: Mongol Fragmentation & Trade Decline
core_concepts.detail: The Mongol Empire's fragmentation into four competing khanates (Yuan Dynasty, Il-Khanate, Chagatai Khanate, Golden Horde) undermined the Pax Mongolica's commercial benefits by reintroducing political barriers along the Silk Roads.

core_concepts.title: Black Death & Trade Route Disease
core_concepts.detail: The Black Death (1340s–1350s) spread rapidly along trade routes from Central Asia through the Islamic world and Europe, killing approximately one-third of Europe's population; it demonstrated how commercial connectivity could transmit catastrophic pathogens alongside goods.

core_concepts.title: Trans-Saharan Gold & Salt Trade
core_concepts.detail: Trans-Saharan trade connected West African gold and salt to North African and Mediterranean markets; camel saddle improvements made Saharan crossings commercially viable, and the Mali Empire grew wealthy by controlling gold production at trade route termini like Timbuktu.

core_concepts.title: Cultural Diffusion Along Trade Routes
core_concepts.detail: Long-distance trade facilitated cultural diffusion alongside commerce: Buddhism spread along Silk Roads, Islam spread via Indian Ocean merchants, gunpowder and paper moved from China westward, and mathematical zero moved from India through the Islamic world to Europe.

exam_tip: The AP exam frequently asks you to compare trade networks and explain why the same exchange system could have both positive consequences (commercial growth, cultural diffusion) and negative ones (disease transmission, disruption from conquest) — practice framing both Mongol political unification and the Black Death as outcomes of the same increased connectivity.
